points: French society under the Old Regime was divided into three estates, with the clergy and nobility enjoying privileges by birth while the third estate bore the entire tax burden.
Louis XVI faced an empty treasury due to war debts, extravagant court expenses, and aid to the American colonies, forcing him to increase taxes.
A subsistence crisis caused by population growth, rising bread prices, and stagnant wages led to widespread discontent among the masses.
Enlightenment philosophers like Locke, Rousseau, and Montesquieu inspired the middle class with ideas of liberty, equality, and division of powers.
The Estates General was convened in May 1789; the third estate formed the National Assembly and took the Tennis Court Oath, vowing to draft a constitution.
On 14 July 1789, Parisians stormed the Bastille, a symbol of royal despotism, marking the beginning of the revolution.
The Great Fear spread in the countryside, leading peasants to attack noble estates; on 4 August 1789, the National Assembly abolished the feudal system.
The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen proclaimed natural and inalienable rights including liberty, property, security, and equality before law.
The Constitution of 1791 established a constitutional monarchy, created an elected National Assembly, and divided citizens into active (voting rights) and passive categories based on tax payment.
In 1792, the Jacobins led an insurrection, the monarchy was abolished, and France was declared a republic; Louis XVI was executed in 1793.
The Reign of Terror (1793-1794) under Robespierre saw severe repression of perceived enemies through the guillotine, rationing, and price controls.
The Directory (1795-1799) was a five-member executive that proved unstable, paving the way for Napoleon Bonaparte's rise to power.

Women actively participated in the revolution, formed political clubs, demanded voting rights, and won temporary gains including compulsory schooling and legal divorce, but the right to vote was granted only in 1946.
Slavery was abolished in French colonies by the Convention in 1794, briefly reintroduced by Napoleon, and finally abolished in 1848.
Censorship was abolished, freedom of the press was established, and revolutionary ideas spread through newspapers, pamphlets, and songs like the Marseillaise.
The French Revolution's ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity became enduring legacies that inspired democratic movements and anti-colonial struggles across Europe and the world.
